Timeflow · Case Study
Tetiana Dovgalenko - AI Design Engineer

Case Study · AI timesheet assistant

Timeflow.
AI fills in the rest.

An AI-powered timesheet assistant that reconstructs your workday from digital signals - so you confirm in 10 seconds, not 10 minutes.

2026 · 4-week build Product Design Frontend + Backend Concept → deployed Solo project

Problem to solve · Why this matters

The problem was already visible

Problem framing for this case study is based on the central hypothesis behind Timeflow: timesheet entry is still manual, memory-based, and error-prone in many team environments. Before designing delight, automation, or polish, the product had to solve a very practical enterprise problem: people were spending too much time reconstructing the week from memory, and the data was still unreliable for planning and billing.

73%
of employees fill timesheets from memory instead of real-time tracking, turning a weekly task into recall work
22min
average time spent per week on timesheet entry, mostly reconstructing the week rather than confirming it
40%
of logged hours are estimated, reducing confidence in billing, planning, and team-capacity data

Built end-to-end as a solo project - from research and strategy through design system, UI, and a deployed full-stack application with real Google Calendar integration. What follows is the path from a single research insight to a working product.

What this could unlock for a 1,000+ person company

367h
of admin time potentially reclaimed every week if 1,000 employees move from 22-minute entry to near-instant confirmation
19kh
of working time potentially returned per year to actual project work instead of reconstructing the week from memory
1hub
A single review flow can unify as many integrations as the workflow needs, while preserving source attribution on every entry

Research & strategy

Finding the real signal

Before designing anything, the goal was to understand what data already exists about how people spend their workday, and what the minimal interaction is to make it useful. Competitive analysis, interviews, and a Jobs-To-Be-Done framework surfaced distinct user moments - and one critical insight: the emotional relationship with timesheets is negative. Anything that felt like surveillance or another chore would be rejected. Privacy controls were designed from day one. Trust is the product.

01 · Research

Problem space analysis

This step turned scattered complaints into a structured problem map. It showed the issue from three angles at once: business risk from late and estimated timesheets, employee frustration with recall-heavy logging, and the tension between employee speed, manager completeness, and finance accuracy. That framing made the design goal much sharper: reduce memory work without creating more pressure.

02 · Research

Research plan & methods

Three sequential phases - generative, evaluative, validating - with a methods matrix mapping each technique to its goal, participants, and rationale.

03 · Research

Competitive analysis

10 time-tracking tools launched 2024-2026, scanned for patterns and gaps across features, pricing, audience, and positioning.

04 · Research

UX literature review

Ten key articles from UX Collective, NN/g and Smashing distilled into core insights and their direct relevance to time tracking.

05 · Research

Synthesized patterns

Six recurring market patterns - AI categorization, macOS-first niching, wellness framing, frictionless entry, dark-vs-glass aesthetics.

06 · Research

Product hypotheses

Two sharp bets: an anti-surveillance tracker for remote teams, and one built for ADHD / neurodivergent professionals.

07 · Research

Jobs-To-Be-Done

Four core jobs mapped with persona, real problem, and obstacles - the Forgetter, the Late-Logger, the Concurrent Logger, the Manager.

08 · Research

Insights & How-Might-We

Four research insights reframed into HMW questions - the lead one: logging is a memory problem, not a motivation problem.

The reframe

Don't log time. Review it.

The reframe was simple but powerful: shift from time tracking - active, continuous effort - to time reviewing - passive collection, one moment of confirmation. Timeflow watches the digital signals that already exist and silently builds a draft timesheet throughout the day. At 17:30 it surfaces that draft. The user's only job is to glance, adjust if needed, and confirm. This single decision determined every screen that followed.

01
AI watches
Calendar, Figma, Jira collect signals silently.
02
Draft built
Claude API groups by project, assigns durations.
03
17:30 ping
"Your day is ready to review."
04
10s review
User confirms, edits, or removes entries.
05
Submitted
Manager sees it instantly. Day closed.

UX principles

A set of non-negotiable design rules was established before a single screen was built. These governed every decision.

✓
Never block Confirm
Even if the AI is uncertain, the user can always submit.
✓
Never use red for overdue
Orange only - red signals error, not delay.
✓
Never say "You missed X days"
Say "X days ready to review" instead.
✓
No "Are you sure?" dialogs
Use Undo instead. Every action is reversible.
✓
Source transparency
Every AI-generated entry shows where it came from.
✓
AI banner disappears after submit
No lingering pressure. The day is closed.

Daily confirmation · The core screen

Review your day - don't log it

The dashboard greets the user with a ready AI draft at the top - “Your AI draft is ready, 6h 45m logged.” Three KPI cards with animated water-fill bubbles show hours today, this week, and projects approved. The entry table lists each block of work with its duration, a confidence label, and a source icon (Figma, Calendar, browser) so the person can trust what they’re confirming. The primary action is a single Review & Submit - confirm, don’t enter.

TaskAt the end of the day, let the employee confirm an AI-built timesheet instead of reconstructing it from memory
BenefitA workday closes in about 10 seconds: the AI draft is already there, each entry shows its source and a confidence level
AI-generated draftConfidence levelsSource attributionWater-fill KPI cardsOne primary action

Data & privacy · Trust is the product

You choose exactly what the AI can see

The Integrations screen lists each connected source - Figma, Jira, Calendar - with green checkmarks for what the AI reads (file names, session time, ticket IDs) and red marks for what it never touches (file content, screenshots, comment text). Granular controls were designed from day one because the research showed the emotional relationship with timesheets is negative: anything that feels like surveillance would be rejected outright.

TaskMake the privacy contract explicit so people trust the AI enough to confirm quickly
BenefitEvery source shows precisely what is read and what is never accessed - privacy is surfaced proactively, not buried in settings
Granular privacy controlsRead vs never-accessedNo screenshots, everProactive transparencyOAuth data scopes

Demo note: integrations are intentionally unavailable to external viewers in the live demo. This protects connected accounts and keeps the public version aligned with the product’s security policy.

Design system

Warm precision. Not another dashboard.

Most productivity tools look cold, clinical, and transactional. Timeflow was designed to feel like a trusted assistant - warm but precise, clear but not sterile. The system is built around a parchment background, the Urbanist typeface, and a constrained palette where teal signals time values, orange signals attention, and ink handles everything else.

A set of non-negotiable rules governed every decision: never use red for overdue, never block submit, show source transparency on every entry, and let orange appear in exactly three places - the topbar alert, the sidebar deadline, and the deadline date. Nowhere else. The manager view went through a full colour audit to remove orange overload.

📜
Parchment, not white#F5F2EC
The whole product sits on a warm parchment background with the Urbanist typeface. Colour is used purposefully, never decoratively - teal for time values, orange for attention, ink for everything structural.
🌊
KPI cards - water-fill animation
Each card uses a water-fill animation to show progress as a percentage - familiar and immediately readable. Secondary values use a unified 18px / weight-500 style at a 4.1:1 contrast ratio, verified against WCAG AA.
🎨
Colour hierarchy - orange in three places
Orange appears only in the topbar alert, the sidebar deadline, and the deadline date. A full colour audit of the manager view cut six simultaneous orange elements down to three.
🗂️
Heatmap - consistent pastel language
The weekly overview heatmap uses the same pastel gradients as the KPI bubbles - mint for full days, light blue for partial, peach for missing - so the visual language stays consistent across the product.

From Figma frames to live product

Designed and built end-to-end

The entire product was built from scratch - research to deployed application - combining product design, frontend engineering, and backend infrastructure. The UI was prototyped in Lovable, then migrated to Codex for production-grade development: the speed of AI-assisted prototyping with the control needed for real integrations.

The core technical milestone was connecting a real third-party API end-to-end: a full OAuth 2.0 flow with calendar.readonly scope, tokens stored securely in Supabase, live events fetched and surfaced on the dashboard with source attribution, and every push to main auto-deploying to production on Vercel.

React + TypeScriptSupabaseVercelGoogle Calendar APIOAuth 2.0RechartsTailwind CSSshadcn/uiClaude API (next)

Design process - from insight to interface

Each phase used a specific tool chosen for its strength - AI for speed where it helped, hand-control where it mattered.

Perplexity
Competitor research - gathering and synthesising data on existing time-tracking tools.
ChatGPT
Competitor research support, building user personas, plus UI/UX review against the design system.
ProductHunt
Competitive market scan - 10 tools analysed for patterns and gaps.
JTBD method
Jobs-To-Be-Done framework to define 4 core jobs instead of personas.
Banani.ai
Wireframing - building the mid-fi screen layouts and flows.
Figma Make
Initial hi-fi prototype generation from a custom Master Prompt + Guidelines.
Design tokens
CSS variables mapped 1:1 to Figma Variables, built on Radix UI + shadcn/ui.

Development process - from prototype to production

Lovable
Design build - scaffolding the React UI and all screens from one master prompt.
Codex
Code - auth flow, Supabase setup, Google Calendar integration, dynamic features, fixes.
Antigravity
Code - additional development and implementation work alongside Codex.
Supabase
Postgres database, authentication, and OAuth token storage.
GitHub
Version control and source for Vercel auto-deploy on every push.
Vercel
Hosting and CI/CD, environment variables, SPA routing config.

Integrations & infrastructure

Connecting a real third-party API end-to-end - OAuth, token storage, and live data - was the core technical milestone.

Auth - Google OAuth via Supabase Auth
Full OAuth 2.0 flow with calendar.readonly scope. Tokens stored securely in the Supabase integrations table with a UNIQUE constraint on (user_id, provider). Handled multiple redirect URI configurations across localhost, Vercel, and the Supabase callback.
Data - real-time Google Calendar integration
getGoogleCalendarEvents() fetches today's events via the googleapis REST API using the stored access token. Events appear on the dashboard with source="Calendar", contributing to the total hours in real time.
Deploy - CI/CD with Vercel + GitHub
Every push to main auto-deploys to production. Environment variables managed in the Vercel dashboard, with vercel.json rewrites configured for React Router SPA routing.

Where it got complicated

Three problems worth keeping

Building Timeflow end-to-end as a solo designer-developer surfaced a different kind of problem-solving than working in a team. Three moments shaped the product most.

🔐

The OAuth 404 chain

After successful Google authorization, the callback kept returning 404. The redirect URIs were correct. The real cause was a missing UNIQUE constraint on the integrations table, which made the Supabase upsert fail silently. Diagnosed by reading raw Auth logs, not guessing.

🎨

Color overload discovery

A screenshot review of the Manager View revealed six simultaneous orange elements competing for attention. A systematic audit cut it back to the three sanctioned uses. Everything else moved to neutral so the hierarchy felt calm again.

⚖️

Trust vs. automation

The deepest tension was behavioral: users need to trust AI enough to confirm quickly, but not so much that they stop checking. The answer was radical transparency. Every entry shows its source and confidence, so trust is earned, not assumed.

Results

Working product. Real data.

In four weeks, Timeflow moved beyond concept into a live, working product. The application is deployed, the authentication flow works end-to-end, and real Google Calendar events already appear inside the interface as attributed entries that update the day’s total automatically.

That moment mattered: it proved the core promise is not hypothetical anymore. Timeflow can already turn existing digital signals into something reviewable, trustworthy, and product-ready. The next step is expanding the signal layer with Claude-generated structuring, but the foundation is already real.

View live demo version

Learnings

What this taught me

AI pushed the design system further

Working with AI took my UI process to a new level. It helped me explore structure faster, but the real value came from turning that speed into consistency: refining the design system, shaping clearer component behaviour, and building animations that felt intentional rather than decorative.

Integrations are product work, not just setup

Connecting Google Calendar was one of the most demanding parts of the build. I spent a significant amount of time learning how the integration layer really works across Google Cloud, OAuth configuration, redirect flows, permissions, and token handling, and that work turned a concept into a functioning product.

Databases became part of the design process

This project taught me how much better design decisions become when I can work directly with data. I learned to set up and structure the database itself, and to create a seeded demo dataset that populated the product with believable content for prototypes and testing instead of manually entering my own data every time.

Admin oversight can feel supportive, not stressful

Designing the admin role taught me that oversight does not have to create pressure. In Timeflow, the admin can independently track what needs attention, but the experience stays friendly, calm, and transparent. The goal was not control for its own sake, but a thoughtful system that helps people stay aligned without feeling monitored.

Tetiana Dovgalenko - UX/UI Designer tetiana.dovgalenko@gmail.com
